title: Prenatal exposure to diabetes or cigarette smoke and postnatal sensitivity to cigarette smoke
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"r79o","text":"Prenatal exposure to maternal diabetes and cigarette smoke have been linked with negative respiratory consequences for offspring, including increasing risk for asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). This project aims to explore how prenatal factors influence offspring sensitivity to cigarette smoke later in life, as a surrogate for COPD risk. Downstream indicators of lung function, inflammation, and tissue for molecular analysis were collected. All the procedures for this study were done in accordance with University of Manitoba Animal Ethics (#21-011). In keeping with the 3R's of animal research, a single control group was used in this study, exposures and data were collected at the same time as the experimental groups. In this way, the control mice act as a relevant comparator for multiple prenatal exposures without necessitating separate control groups. There are four prenatal exposure groups generated from this protocol: Control (low-fat diet and room air), Maternal Diabetes (high-fat diet and room air), Maternal Smoking (low-fat diet and cigarette smoke), and Maternal Diabetes with Smoking (high-fat diet and cigarette smoke). The two aims of this project were to address 1) whether individual prenatal factors influence offspring sensitivity to cigarette smoke, 2) if prenatal exposure interact to influence influence cigarette smoke sensitivity. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Use six-week-old female mice (C57BL/6NJ) for diet exposure. Randomly assign mice into groups. 1. Maximum 4 female mice per cage. 2. Add environmental enrichments to the cage.
Glucose Tolerance Test
Maternal diets continue throughout the breeding, pregnancy and until the offspring get weaned at three-weeks of age.
Use 7-8 weeks old male C57BL/6NJ mice for breeding.
Bronchoalveolar Lavage
Prepare ice-cold PBS (1 mL per wash). Set up 1 mL syringes. Label collection tubes (2 mL tubes) and keep them on ice.
Blood
Lung Tissue

At 8-weeks of age, weigh the mice prior to starting acute cigarette smoke exposure. This acute cigarette smoke exposure protocol is designed to test the sensitivity of mice to cigarette smoke induced airway inflammation and lung function.
Prenatal backgrounds generated by the exposure protocols presented. CS - Cigarette Smoke, MD - Maternal Diabetes. Both male and female offspring used for downstream measures of lung health.

Fast mice overnight (18 hrs) before GTT. Fasting starts in the evening prior to the test date (around 4 pm). Remove food from cage, keep water bottles overnight during fasting hours. Weigh the food and save it to give back to the mice after GTT. If food is present in bedding of the cage, move animals to a new cage to ensure no food is consumed during fasting.
Freshly prepare 10% glucose solution. Weigh 1g of D-Glucose and dissolve it in 10 mL of distilled water. Mix well by vortexing. Filter sterilize 10% glucose solution using 0.22 µm syringe filter.

Weigh mouse and record body weight for dose calculation. Calculate dose of glucose for injection (10 µL/g). Dose Calculation: Volume of 10% Glucose (µL) = 10 µL/g × Body weight of mouse (g) Prepare 1mL syringes and 26G needle with calculated volume of glucose, removing air bubbles.

Measure fasting/basal blood glucose level at time 'ZERO'. Blood glucose can be measured using commercially available glucose meters and test strips. For example: Contour® Next, model #7359 Contour® Next strips 100x

Perform intraperitoneal injection (IP) with appropriate volume of glucose solution. 1. Restrain the mouse. 2. Identify the appropriate IP injection site. 3. Insert needle at 45 degree angle into the right lower abdominal quadrant. 4. Before injection, pull back the plunger to make sure the needle has not penetrated through bladder, Bowel or blood vessel. 5. Inject and return mouse back to the cage.
Measure and record blood glucose levels for each mouse at specific time intervals post injection: 10, 20, 30, 60 and 120 minutes, using blood from tail vein or saphenous vein.
Return mice back to their cages with food and environmental enrichments to the cages.

Plot time (minutes) versus blood glucose level (mmol/L) to assess glucose tolerance. Blood glucose concentration should spike 10-20 minutes post IP injection and return to baseline within 120 minutes.
Diet Exposure

Add 250g of high-fat diet ( High-fat 45% kcal diet Research Diets Inc Catalog #D12451 ) into MD group cages to induce body weight gain and glucose intolerance as per published diet induced maternal diabetes model. Pereira TJ, Moyce BL, Kereliuk SM, Dolinsky VW.
Add 250g of low-fat diet ( Low-fat 10% kcal diet Research Diets Inc Catalog #D12450B ) to the control cages.
Weekly: Record food consumption by weighing remaining food and replenish to 250g. Record body weight. Monitor and record body condition, behaviour, and physical signs of distress in each mouse.
At week 6 of diet, perform glucose tolerance test (GTT) go to step #3
Record initial body weight of mice as week 0.

Using lance supplied with glucose monitor, lightly poke mouse tail along side to generate small drop of blood for testing. Alternatively, perform blood sampling from saphenous vein. Do not put significant pressure on the tail, this process should be painless and not require manipulation of the tail.
Split female mice into two groups: Control room air exposure (foreign cage) Cigarette smoke (CS) exposure.
Female mice breeding begins roughly at 12 weeks of age.
House two females with one male per cage for 10 days. Male breeders are maintained on a standard research chow diet, except when they paired with females for breeding purpose.
Check for the presence of vaginal plugs daily in the morning.

Track gestational weight gain every other day. Gestational weight gain can be used to confirm pregnancy in mice even when vaginal plug is not detected. Weight gain exceeding 4 grams likely indicates successful pregnancy. Heyne GW, Plisch EH, Melberg CG, Sandgren EP, Peter JA, Lipinski RJ (2015). A Simple and Reliable Method for Early Pregnancy Detection in Inbred Mice.. https://doi.org/ If females are not pregnant after 10 days, they can be rebred following the same protocol. This will increase the duration of diet and/or cigarette exposures, and so should be avoided if possible so as to not introduce additional variables into the experiment.
Once pregnancy is confirmed, separate two females into individual cages before birth of pups. Provide fresh water and food.
Cull litters to a maximum of six per litter. This mice can be used for postnatal day 1 tissue collection.
Pups are kept with the dam for 3-weeks. Maternal exposures (diet or cigarette smoke) are maintained throughout these 3-weeks.
Wean offspring at 3-weeks of age and house according to sex. Provide standard research chow. Be sure to note which prenatal exposure background they originated from.
Record weaned offspring body weights and sex.
Split each litter into two groups. One group for control room air and the other on for CS exposure.

CS mice both males and females expose to whole body CS for 50 minutes twice a day, four days a week. [Used Scireq In Expose smoking robot (SCIREQ, Montreal, QC, Canada)]. - Use Standard 3R4F research cigarettes (University of Kentucky, Lexington, KY) - 10 cigarettes per exposure (total 20 cigarettes per day). - Flow rate of 2 L/min and one puff per minute, with 5 puffs per cigarette. - Each mouse expose to controlled amount of CS.
In parallel, expose control mice to room air for the same length of time to imitate the stress associates with the exposure.
Cover mouse cage/animal chamber with a green cloth during exposures to reduce animal stress and anxiety.
After exposures, return mice back to their cages with food and water.
Following lung function measurements don’t remove the Catheter from the trachea.
Set up syringes with 1 mL of PBS and gently instill into lung through the tracheal cannula. - Avoid forceful pushes to prevent lung damage.

After instillation, gently aspirate. Repeat instillation and aspiration one more time to collect the lavage fluid. - Usually can recover 60-80% of the volume - This fluid is your broncho alveolar fluid (BALF).
Repeat this wash for 2-times with the same volume (1 mL per wash). - Pool the samples and keep on ice.
Once finish the sample collection, centrifuge samples at 4 °C for 10 minutes at 1100 rpm.
Transfer cell-free supernatant to a new 2 mL vial and aliquotesto 250-500 μL. Store these aliquotes at -80 °C for later analysis.
Resuspend the pellet in 1 mL of flow buffer (PBS with 0.5% BSA) to use for cell differential assessment.

Weekly: Record food consumption by weighing remaining food and replenish to 250g, as describe in go to step #2.5 . Record body weight. Monitor and record body condition, behaviour, and physical signs of distress in each mouse throughout exposure.
After exposures, return mice back to their cages with food and environmental enrichments.
Animals are exposed to room air or cigarette smoke for 50 minutes.
Have a spare, foreign cage available to act as the room air exposure for control animals.
Begin cigarette smoke exposure in female mice 2-weeks prior to breeding. If paired with maternal diet exposures, this would be week 4 of the diet exposure.
Cigarette smoke exposure to be completed using the Scireq inExpose smoking robot and whole body chamber.
Load 10 Standard 3R4F Research Cigarettes into the smoking robot.
Place mice (up to 12) into whole body exposure chamber.

In the FlexiWare software, set the smoking parameters for the smoking robot: Flow rate: 2L/min 1 puff per minute 5 puffs per cigarette Monitor the cigarettes in the robot to ensure they are lit as the robot moves through the cigarettes.
go to step #4.3 Repeat exposures (4.3 to 4.6) twice a day. These are typically done in the morning and afternoon.
Place room air mice in foreign cage to mimic stress associated with new environment of whole body exposure chamber.
Female mice are exposed to cigarette smoke 5 times a week for 2 weeks prior to breeding, throughout breeding, pregnancy, and weaning. Roughly 8-9 weeks total.
Offspring will be housed without environmental exposures until 8-weeks of age.
Cigarette smoke exposure protocol follows same methodology as 4.3 to 4.8 go to step #4.3
Mice are exposed to cigarette smoke twice a day, for 50 minutes, for four days total.
Outcomes and tissue collection are done 24 hours after the last cigarette smoke exposure.
On week 6, following completion of the diet and CS exposure, and confirmation of glucose intolerance, breed mice to generate offspring.
Record the body weights of each mouse at 8-weeks of age.
Anesthetize mice using sodium pentobarbital (90 mg/kg, i.p).
Prepare ice-cold Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and load syringes with 1mL of PBS.
Pinch and lift the abdominal skin with forceps, then cut through it to expose the rib cage. Pull the skin aside to keep fur away from the body.

Expose CS female mice to whole body CS using Scireq In-Expose smoking robot (SCIREQ, Montreal, QC, Canada) as follows: 1. Use standard 3R4F research cigarettes (University of Kentucky, Lexington, KY). 2. Use 10 cigarettes for 50 minutes per exposure. 3. CS exposure twice daily (Total 20 cigarettes per day), 5 days per week. 4. Use flow rate of 2 L/min and one puff per minute, with 5 puffs per cigarette. 5. Confirm lighting of cigarettes in smoking robot. 6. Smoke according to ISO 3308 Standard for mainstream smoke.
Expose control female mice to room air in a spare foreign cage, for the same length of time to mimic the stress associate with the exposure.
Cover mouse foreign cages/smoke chamber with a green cloth during exposures to reduce animal stress and anxiety.
After each cigarette smoke exposure return mice back to their cages with food, water and environmental enrichments to the cages.

Weekly: Record food consumption by weighing remaining food and replenish to 250g as described in step #2.5. Record body weight. Monitor and record body condition, behaviour, and physical signs of distress in each mouse during exposures.

Maternal CS exposure continues throughout breeding, pregnancy and until the offspring get weaned at three-weeks of age. Offspring are never directly exposed to cigarette smoke (ie. never put into the smoke exposure chamber). They are only ever exposed indirectly through pregnancy or during breastfeeding (ie. third hand exposure).

Use 7-8 weeks old C57BL/6NJ male mice for breeding. Breeders are purchased from Jackson Laboratory. Maintain male breeders on a standard research chow diet, except when they paired with females for breeding purpose.
For mating, house two females with one male per cage.
House mice together for 10 days, moving male mice to separate cage on day 10. Keep two female mice together in the same cage.

Track gestational weight gain every other day to confirm their pregnancy status. Plot gestational day versus gestational weight gain (g) to confirm their pregnancy. With a weight gain exceeding 4 grams indicates successful pregnancy. Heyne GW, Plisch EH, Melberg CG, Sandgren EP, Peter JA, Lipinski RJ (2015). A Simple and Reliable Method for Early Pregnancy Detection in Inbred Mice.. Journal of the American Association for Laboratory Animal Science : JAALAS. https://doi.org/

Optional: If females are not pregnant, you can choose to rebreed them with the same male for additional 10 days. This should be noted for downstream data analysis as mice will be exposed to diet and CS for longer than non-rebred mice.
Near the end of gestation, but before females give birth, separate two females into individual cages. Provide fresh water and food.
Check the cages every morning for litters. Take care not to disturb the litters.
Keep pups with dam for 3-weeks until weaning. Diet and CS exposures continue throughout weaning as noted in 2.7 and 4.8
After pups are born, cull litters to a maximum of six per litter. Use excess pups to collect tissues (Ex: milk spots and lung tissues) for future downstream analysis.
At weaning (3-weeks of age) separate offspring from dam and house according to sex. Males and females in separate cages. Weigh the weaned offspring and record their body weights and sex.

At this point introduce offspring to a standard research chow diet. Continue diet until they reach 8-weeks of age. During this 5-week period mice are not expose to any experimental interventions. NO cigarette smoke exposures and NO specific diet interventions.
Split each cage into two more cages. 1) Control group, exposed to room air. 2) Cigarette smoke group.
Cigarette smoke group undergo whole body cigarette smoke exposure for 50 minutes, twice daily, four days. Perform exposures using Scireq inExpose smoking robot as explained in go to step #4.3
Expose control mice to room air in a spare foreign cage, for the same length of time to mimic the stress associate with the exposure.
Make a small incision in the skin of the neck and expose the trachea using forceps and scissors.
Ventilate mice with a tidal volume of 10 ml/kg of body weight, at a rate of 150 breaths per minute.

Capture desired lung function outcomes on flexiVent. This can include PV-Loops, inspiratory capacity, total lung resistance (Rrs), Compliance (C), Elastance (E), Newtonian resistance (Rn), Tissue damping (G), and Tissue elastance (H). Refer to Scireq measurement guidelines for direction.
Remove mouse from flexiVent and euthanize for sample collection. Keep catheter in trachea for sample collection.
Gently and with consistent pressure, instill PBS into lung through the tracheal cannula. Avoid forceful or uneven instillation to prevent lung damage.
After instillation, gently aspirate. Repeat instillation and aspiration one more time to collect lung lavage fluid. You can expect to typically recover ~80% of the instilled volume.
Repeat this wash for one more time using 1 mL of ice-cold PBS. Collect and pool both washes and keep them on ice.
Measure and record the total recovered BAL volume.
Centrifuge all BAL samples. 1100 rpm, 4 °C, 10 minutes
Transfer cell-free supernatant to a new 2 mL vial and make 250-500 µL aliquots. Store these aliquots at -80 °C for future analysis. Careful not to disturb the cell pellet.
Resuspend the cell pellet in 1 mL of FACS buffer (PBS with 0.5% Bovine Serum Albumin, BSA).
Use 10 µL of cell suspension to count total cells/mL using hemocytometer. Expect an average of ~50,000 cells per mL in the control group.
Cover mouse foreign cages/smoke chamber with a green cloth during exposures to reduce animal stress and anxiety.
After each cigarette smoke exposure return mice back to their cages with food, water and environmental enrichments to the cages.

Lung function is measured using the Scireq flexiVent system ~18 hours after the last cigarette smoke exposure. Prior to measuring lung function in mice, complete recommended setup and calibration as per Scireq guidelines.
Carefully insert a 20-gauge polyethylene catheter into the trachea and connect animal to the flexiVent small animal ventilator via catheter.
Weigh and record body weight of each mouse.
Label 2 mL collection tubes and keep them on ice.
Remaining cell pellet can be used for differential cell analysis by flow cytometry.
Gently separate the left and right lung using small, sharp scissors.
Left lung lobes (DNA & Protein)
Mince the left lung lobes into smaller pieces using sterile scissors.
Transfer tissue into 2 mL collection tubes.
Immediately snap-freeze in liquid nitrogen.
Store samples at -80 °C until use for DNA and protein extraction. Avoid repeated freeze-thaw cycles.
Right lung lobes (RNA)
Mince the right lung lobes into smaller pieces using sterile scissors.
Place tissue into a 2 mL tube containing 1 mL of RNAlater‱ solution (Invitrogen, Thermo Fisher Scientific).
Keep samples at 4 °C for 24 hrs.
Transfer tubes to -80 °C for long term storage until use for RNA extractions. Avoid repeated freeze-thaw cycles.
Using fine scissors, make a small cut in the abdominal artery. Collect blood using a sterile syringe, as needed.
Cut through the ventral rib cage, starting from diaphragm and continuing up to thoracic cavity.
Using scissors and forceps, carefully reveal the lungs. Take care to avoid puncturing the lungs.
With the rib cage exposed, make a small abdominal incision below the diaphragm and ribs.
Check each morning for the presence of a vaginal plug to confirm mating.
Outcomes (lung function and sample collection) are performed on day 5, ~18-hours after last CS exposure.
creator: Christopher D Pascoe
